So it really is about half the time a quote box and about half the time a little ad. The opening monologue is probably the closest analogy of the various inspirations for it, is the opening monologue of a talk show host. And more regious than say, Leno, who's doing jokes. But if you go watch the Regis film and show for five or ten days in a row, you'll find he has a pretty consistent format for an opening monologue. There's usually a Regis kind of self-deprecating. I was crapped on, don't they know who I am story? There's some contemporary news event. There's some rant about something he's aggravated about. He's got a little kind of template that he fits things into, and it hasn't changed in at least 30 years. Therefore, we must assume it's kept him on the air for 30 years and ultimately made him a very rich fellow. So there's something to that. The old, if you go back, pre-Karson, the Jackpaw opening monologue was more like this than it is joke, joke, joke, joke, joke. Johnny really switched the nighttime monologue into a joke, joke, joke, joke, joke. So I have my opening monologue in which I'm trying to do some or all of these things. Most important is to establish I'm talking to them in a personal voice and reconnect. You're getting a letter from Dan. Tell some kind of personal story, link to a current event, which is not necessarily easy because obviously there's a lag time with printed communications as you know, so current events become uncurrent events. Be humorous and then make a major point that usually is thematic for the issue. Sometimes this issue doesn't have it, but you certainly have seen them where there's a banner that interrupts the copy on the front page, midway down the page. So when I use the banner, I'm either promoting something, I'm calling attention to some selected thing within the newsletter, and it is usually a really funny, provocative, weird thing in the banner. So it'll be, you know, 15 Naked Elves Celebrate Christmas in Norway, story on page three. It'll be that sort of a thing. Then we start the actual newsletter, if you will, after the monologue. And those are short to medium length items. And what I strive for in those is references when I can to things they're familiar with. So Disney, Starbucks, FedEx, not some company they never heard of. Even if I got a good story about a company they never heard of, I usually won't use it. Or I'll go try and find a story that makes the same point but involves something they're familiar with. Member related or provided. This particular issue, September, that I gave you here, happens to be a little light on that. And I don't remember why now, but other issues are really heavy on it. So all of this varies a little bit issue to issue, but I try to hit as many of them as I can in every issue. And when I say member related or provided, it doesn't much matter to me if it's a story about a member, a member's example, or even just recognizing the member who sent me something to see. One's about as good as the other, because the point of it is recognition more so than anything else. So, and you know, we talked about it a little bit yesterday. I mean, we underdo this recognition thing. And you really want to try and do as much of it as you can, every place that you can. Specifically to inspire the behavior you want and generally just to reward people for being around. You know, I used to joke, and it's only partially funny, because it's instructive. Mary Kay probably of all the direct sales companies does a better job of it than anybody, but they all have to do it. But I mean, you know, the Mary Kay deal is if you show up at a meeting and breathe, you get a ribbon. If you show up three times in a row, you get a car. I mean, and there's a button and there's a badge and there's a ribbon for everything. And almost all direct sales companies revolve around that. Why? Because it works. That's why. Especially in an environment where they ain't making any money. But it works in every environment. And so we don't have the same, we're not getting together with them every week to give them awards, but we can give them a recognition and print. On that, we're thinking about putting like a six month certificate or completion thing. Is there any thought to including that in the newsletter when they hit that mark? Yeah, you published now the list of who got the three month consistency pin, who got the six month consistency pin, who got the... You know, I grew up in that environment. And so it started with consistency being roughly equivalent to one days of consistency. I mean, and it went from there, you know, and gradually went from paper and ink certificates to plaques to, you know. We actually had at $750,000 and $1,500 in personal sales in Amway. When I was in Amway, you got this pretty nice little wood plaque with the logo on it, then the 750. We had so many of them, we used it as a parquet floor in the basement. We parquet floor in the basement with these things. But you wanted the next one and you wanted the next one and you wanted the next one. So I try and recognize both lifers, long timers, sort of as a reward for them being around and spread the recognition around as best I can. And there's lots of ways to get at it. So like mail is one way to get at it. And so at least probably four out of every 12 newsletters has a Dan gets mail section and if this one doesn't. And I'll respond to three or four or five letters that I've gotten from people. Well, that gives me an opportunity to do several things. It gives me an opportunity to display testimonials in a different context. So they're not in a box that says testimonials at the top. You know, you get to kind of sneak them up on people. It gives me a chance to give recognition to four or five people. It gives me a chance to make a point in response to what they sent in. You know, so I'm always looking for ways to give the member. And so you can think about what you're describing has like formal recognition, right? You can think about what I'm describing has more informal recognition. And generally speaking, the more of what you do, I think the better off you are. I try. I think our video. Are we missing any numbers past nine? Nine. And if you can solve it. I think I have a bunch of numbers. Yeah. Twenty-nine, yeah. Thirty. Oh, we're going to get there. No. All of those numbers are your original list, one through thirty-one. Okay. So you have an alpha list here, which is what I'm talking about now. Your numerical list is your one through thirty-one. Okay. Where the hell was I? Oh, I try and be funny. Why be funny? Because people like to be entertained. And people prefer comedy to serious stuff. And the old gag into speaking business was, do you have to be funny only if you want to be paid? And that's really pretty much the truth, right? The academic Harvard professor who dispenses information rarely gets invited back. The guy who makes him laugh could get booked to come back next year at the end of the engagement. There's some sort of rant or personal experience kind of thing in there. There's some tips. So there is some like useful content. Not a lot, but some. I try and reinforce the key elements of what we teach. So again and again and again. Long copy versus short copy, good headlines, a pile mail. More importantly, reinforce the behavior we need from them. So these items are often worked into them as reinforcement of good behavior. Being a good student, reading intellectual curiosity, seeking out information, so forth. Work ethic. Coming to events. Having a coach, ascending, etc. And I try and give them examples of extreme stupidity that are not from our members in most cases, but are from non-member companies and individuals that allow them to feel superior because the stupidity is so extreme that no one could possibly commit the sins described. The big lesson.
